root_text.an5.71:1.1: “Pañcime, bhikkhave, dhammā bhāvitā bahulīkatā cetovimuttiphalā ca honti cetovimuttiphalānisaṁsā ca, paññāvimuttiphalā ca honti paññāvimuttiphalānisaṁsā ca.
root_text.an5.71:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu asubhānupassī kāye viharati, āhāre paṭikūlasaññī, sabbaloke anabhiratasaññī, sabbasaṅkhāresu aniccānupassī, maraṇasaññā kho panassa ajjhattaṁ sūpaṭṭhitā hoti.
root_text.an5.71:2.3: Ime kho, bhikkhave, pañca dhammā bhāvitā bahulīkatā cetovimuttiphalā ca honti cetovimuttiphalānisaṁsā ca, paññāvimuttiphalā ca honti paññāvimuttiphalānisaṁsā ca.
root_text.an5.71:2.4: Yato kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu cetovimutto ca hoti paññāvimutto ca hoti—
root_text.an5.71:2.5: ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ‘bhikkhu ukkhittapaligho itipi, saṅkiṇṇaparikho itipi, abbūḷhesiko itipi, niraggaḷo itipi, ariyo pannaddhajo pannabhāro visaṁyutto itipi’.
root_text.an5.71:3.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno avijjā pahīnā hoti ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.an5.71:3.3: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ukkhittapaligho hoti.
root_text.an5.71:4.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno ponobhaviko jātisaṁsāro pahīno hoti ucchinnamūlo tālāvatthukato anabhāvaṅkato āyatiṁ anuppādadhammo.
root_text.an5.71:4.3: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu saṅkiṇṇaparikho hoti.
root_text.an5.71:5.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno taṇhā pahīnā hoti ucchinnamūlā tālāvatthukatā anabhāvaṅkatā āyatiṁ anuppādadhammā.
root_text.an5.71:5.3: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu abbūḷhesiko hoti.
root_text.an5.71:6.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno pañcorambhāgiyāni saṁyojanāni pahīnāni honti ucchinnamūlāni tālāvatthukatāni anabhāvaṅkatāni āyatiṁ anuppādadhammāni.
root_text.an5.71:6.3: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu niraggaḷo hoti.
root_text.an5.71:7.1: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ariyo pannaddhajo pannabhāro visaṁyutto hoti?
root_text.an5.71:7.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno asmimāno pahīno hoti ucchinnamūlo tālāvatthukato anabhāvaṅkato āyatiṁ anuppādadhammo.
root_text.an5.71:7.3: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ariyo pannaddhajo pannabhāro visaṁyutto hotī”ti.

translation_text.an5.71:0.3: Freedom of Heart is the Fruit (1st)
translation_text.an5.71:1.1: “Mendicants, these five things, when developed and cultivated, have freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom as their fruit and benefit.
translation_text.an5.71:2.2: A mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions, and has well established the perception of their own death.
translation_text.an5.71:2.3: These five things, when developed and cultivated, have freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom as their fruit and benefit.
translation_text.an5.71:2.4: When a mendicant has freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom,
translation_text.an5.71:2.5: such a mendicant is one who is called ‘one who has lifted the cross-bar’, ‘one who has filled in the moat’, ‘one who has pulled up the pillar’, ‘one who is unimpeded’, and also ‘a noble one with banner lowered and burden dropped, detached’.
translation_text.an5.71:3.1: And how has a mendicant lifted the cross-bar?
translation_text.an5.71:3.2: It’s when a mendicant has given up ignorance, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.an5.71:3.3: That’s how a mendicant has lifted the cross-bar.
translation_text.an5.71:4.1: And how has a mendicant filled in the moat?
translation_text.an5.71:4.2: It’s when a mendicant has given up transmigrating through births in future lives, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.an5.71:4.3: That’s how a mendicant has filled in the moat.
translation_text.an5.71:5.1: And how has a mendicant pulled up the pillar?
translation_text.an5.71:5.2: It’s when a mendicant has given up craving, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.an5.71:5.3: That’s how a mendicant has pulled up the pillar.
translation_text.an5.71:6.1: And how is a mendicant unimpeded?
translation_text.an5.71:6.2: It’s when a mendicant has given up the five lower fetters, cut them off at the root, made them like a palm stump, obliterated them, so they’re unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.an5.71:6.3: That’s how a mendicant is unimpeded.
translation_text.an5.71:7.1: And how is a mendicant a noble one with with banner lowered and burden dropped, detached?
translation_text.an5.71:7.2: It’s when a mendicant has given up the conceit ‘I am’, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, obliterated it, so it’s unable to arise in the future.
translation_text.an5.71:7.3: That’s how a mendicant is a noble one with banner lowered and burden dropped, detached.”

variant_text.an5.71:2.4: paññāvimutto ca hoti → paññāvimutto ca (bj, sya-all, pts1ed)
